briefs.title: Council to vote on blocking BC Housing complex care project at 5389 Imperial Street
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on a motion that would commit the City to not support, approve, or endorse a BC Housing supportive and complex care housing development at 5389 Imperial Street. Councillor Lee brought forward the motion, citing a high volume of resident emails and widespread community concern about the proposed location and the adequacy of public consultation.
briefs.action_required: Attend the February 10, 2026 Council meeting to have your say before the vote.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council vote — BC Housing 5389 Imperial Street motion

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Healthcare

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to Consider Imperial Oil Petition — Details Redacted
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to consider a petition (a formal written request) submitted by Imperial Oil, covering pages 221–302 of the agenda package dated February 10, 2026. The full content of the petition has been redacted — meaning the specific details have been removed from the public document — so the exact nature of the request is not publicly known at this time.
briefs.action_required: Monitor Burnaby Council meeting updates for details on this petition as more information becomes available.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council meeting — Imperial Oil petition consideration date

briefs.title: Council to Consider Petition Opposing Supportive Housing Project at 5389 Imperial Street
briefs.summary: Council will consider a supplementary petition — a formal written request signed by residents — from the Burnaby Neighbourhood Watch opposing a proposed BC Housing supportive and complex care housing development at 5389 Imperial Street. The petition carries 981 additional signatures, on top of 11,749 collected earlier, asking Council to reject the project.
briefs.action_required: Attend the February 10, 2026 Council meeting to follow the discussion on this housing proposal.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council meeting — supplementary petition on 5389 Imperial St housing considered

briefs.title: Council to vote on Burnaby's five-year financial plan covering 2026 to 2030
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would adopt the city's five-year financial plan for 2026 to 2030. The plan outlines how the city expects to raise and spend money each year, with total proposed revenues starting at roughly $873 million in 2026 and rising to over $1 billion by 2030 — funded mainly through property taxes, which would make up about 45% of revenue.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council vote — property tax increases in this plan could affect your business costs.

key_dates.label: Burnaby 2026–2030 Financial Plan Bylaw — council adoption vote

briefs.industries: Finance & Insurance, Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt

briefs.title: Council to Consider Update on Burnaby's Online Recreation Registration System Improvements
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to receive an information update on improvements made to WebReg, the city's online recreation registration system, since it launched in 2023. The update covers changes like rolling registration windows for swim and skate lessons, a new flexible monthly pass option, removal of annual pass holds, and better tools to ensure fair access for Burnaby residents.
briefs.action_required: If you use Burnaby recreation facilities, review the new pass options and updated registration rules at the City's website.

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Burnaby's 10 Park Development Projects for 2026–2027
briefs.summary: Council is set to receive a report outlining 10 park development projects planned for 2026–2027 in Burnaby. The projects — already included in the 2026–2030 capital plan — include playground replacements, a new basketball court, spray park design, artificial turf replacement, trail management, and signage upgrades across several Burnaby parks.
briefs.action_required: Check the City of Burnaby's project webpage and social media for feedback surveys if a project is near your business.

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Burnaby Parks, Recreation and Culture Plan — Community Feedback Report
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to consider a 'What We Heard' report summarizing community feedback gathered in summer 2025 for the proposed Making Life Better Parks, Recreation and Culture Plan. The report — which has not yet been approved — captures input from over 3,000 residents on parks, recreation facilities, and cultural services, and will be used to shape a long-term plan for how Burnaby manages and improves these services going forward.
briefs.action_required: Review the report and watch for the next public engagement phase in early 2026 to have your say.

key_dates.label: Making Life Better PRC Plan — key directions shared with public, early 2026

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Burnaby Parks, Recreation & Culture Plan Moves to Drafting Phase
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to receive an update on the Parks, Recreation and Culture (PRC) Plan, which is now entering Phase 3 — the drafting stage. Staff will pull together research and public feedback to build a draft plan covering 15 service areas. A second round of public engagement is planned for March 2026, where residents and community groups can review and comment on the draft before it is finalized later in spring 2026.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the March 2026 public engagement — you can review the draft plan and share feedback at Burnaby.ca/PRCPlan.

key_dates.label: Burnaby PRC Plan Phase 4 public engagement opens — comment period

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Burnaby Building Permit Activity Report — December 2025
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to review the Building Permit Activity Report for December 2025, which tracks construction activity across the city. The report shows 132 permits issued in December alone, totalling over $152 million in construction value, and a year-to-date total of 1,225 permits worth more than $3.15 billion — a significant jump from prior years.
briefs.action_required: Review the report to understand construction trends in Burnaby that may affect your business or property plans.

briefs.title: Council to receive Burnaby building permit activity report for December 2025
briefs.summary: Council is set to receive a routine information report covering all building permits issued in Burnaby during December 2025. No vote or new rules are involved — Council will simply acknowledge the report, which tracks local construction activity for that month. The full report will be posted on the City of Burnaby website after the February 10, 2026 meeting.
briefs.action_required: Check the City of Burnaby website after Feb 10, 2026 for the published permit activity data.

briefs.title: Council to vote on $10,000 for staff to attend 2026 BCRPA Symposium in Vancouver
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on spending up to $10,000 to send up to six members of the Parks, Recreation and Culture Committee to the 2026 BCRPA Symposium in Vancouver, running April 28 – May 1, 2026. The BCRPA (British Columbia Recreation and Parks Association) is a not-for-profit group that supports parks and recreation across the province, and this is its main annual event.

key_dates.label: 2026 BCRPA Symposium takes place — Vancouver

briefs.title: Council to Consider: 2-Year Renewal of Road Marking Paint and Glass Bead Contract
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a second renewal of the City's contract for road marking paint and glass beads — the materials used to paint lines and markings on Burnaby streets. The proposed renewal would run from March 1, 2026 to February 29, 2028 (24 months) at an estimated value of $380,000, with no increase in the per-unit price compared to the previous term.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision if you supply road marking materials or related services to the City.

key_dates.label: Burnaby road marking paint contract 2nd renewal — proposed start date
Burnaby road marking paint contract 2nd renewal — proposed end date

briefs.title: Council to vote on $380,000 contract renewal for road marking paint and glass beads
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on renewing a supply contract with Cloverdale Paint Inc. for road marking paint and glass beads — materials used to keep lane lines and pavement markings visible across the city. The proposed two-year renewal is valued at an estimated $380,000 including taxes, with the supplier agreeing to hold prices steady to protect against market swings.
briefs.action_required: No action required for most businesses — this is a city supply contract with no direct impact on private owners.

briefs.title: Council to consider renewing Burnaby's fence rental contract for 2026–2027
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on renewing a fence rental contract for one more year, from June 1, 2026 to May 31, 2027, at a proposed cost of $267,000 — a 6.8% increase over the current term. If approved, the total value of this contract since it began in 2022 would reach an estimated $766,000 including tax.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision if you supply fencing or compete for city rental contracts.

key_dates.label: Burnaby fence rental contract 4th renewal proposed start date
Burnaby fence rental contract 4th renewal proposed end date

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Transportation & Logistics, Professional Services

briefs.title: Council to vote on $267,000 fence rental contract renewal with Super Save
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on renewing its temporary fence rental contract with Super Save Fence Rentals Inc. for one final year (June 1, 2026 to May 31, 2027) at an estimated cost of $267,000 including taxes. The fencing supports city events, construction sites, and public safety needs — and the current contract ran out early due to higher-than-expected demand.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision on Feb 10 if you supply fencing or compete for city contracts.

key_dates.label: Burnaby fence rental contract renewal term starts — Super Save agreement
Burnaby fence rental contract renewal term ends — Super Save agreement

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Transportation & Logistics, Professional Services

briefs.title: Council to vote on $7.19M contract extension for Burnaby police dispatch services
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on extending its contract with E-COMM Emergency Communications For BC Incorporated for one year (January–December 2025) at an estimated cost of $7,194,789. The extension would keep police dispatch services running while a provincial review of E-COMM is completed and a new long-term agreement is drafted for Council's approval.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — this affects public safety service costs in Burnaby's 2025 budget.

key_dates.label: E-COMM police dispatch contract extension period ends — Burnaby

briefs.title: Council to vote on: Proposed $7.3M renewal of Burnaby's E-COMM 911 dispatch contract
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on renewing its contract with E-COMM — the regional 911 emergency communications centre — for another year (January to December 2026) at a cost of $7.3 million. This proposed renewal (#5) represents a 1% increase over the previous year and would bring the total value of all E-COMM contracts since 2013 to roughly $55.3 million.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council vote — this contract funds Burnaby's 911 dispatch services for 2026.

key_dates.label: E-COMM Contract Renewal #5 proposed start date — Burnaby 911 dispatch
E-COMM Contract Renewal #5 proposed end date — Burnaby 911 dispatch

briefs.title: Council to vote on $7.3M renewal of Burnaby police dispatch contract with E-COMM
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on renewing its police dispatch services contract with E-COMM Emergency Communications for BC Incorporated for one year (January 1 to December 31, 2026) at an estimated cost of $7,300,000. The proposed renewal keeps the same terms as the existing contract while the City continues reviewing E-COMM's service levels — and comes in $500,000 lower than earlier estimates due to fewer calls, reduced hiring, and cost-saving measures.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — this contract affects public safety services funded through Burnaby's 2026 budget.

key_dates.label: Burnaby E-COMM police dispatch contract renewal period begins
Burnaby E-COMM police dispatch contract renewal period ends

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Pending Contract Change for Hazardous Material Survey Services
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a pending contract change that would add $240,000 to an existing hazardous material survey contract, bringing the total estimated contract value to $558,800 over five years. This change would expand the contractor's work to support the City's Civic Projects division, on top of the Facilities division already covered.
briefs.action_required: Monitor the council decision if you provide hazardous material survey or environmental consulting services to municipalities.

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to approve $240,000 contract increase for hazardous building material surveys
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a $240,000 contract change (an increase to an existing agreement) with Astech Consultants Ltd. to carry out hazardous building material surveys at City-owned properties before demolition work begins. The extra funding is needed because more houses are being demolished than originally planned, requiring more pre-demolition inspections to check for dangerous materials like asbestos.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — this could affect demolition or environmental consulting contracts in Burnaby.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council meeting — hazardous building material survey contract vote

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Burnaby BAH Program Contract Renewal #3 and Optional Cost Increase
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on renewing a service contract for the BAH (Business Assistance/Housing — exact program name abbreviated) program for 2026, and on an optional contract change that would add $193,000 — a 3.8% increase — to the contract. The total estimated value of this contract since it started in 2022 would reach approximately $807,000 if both items are approved.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the Council decision on February 10, 2026 — it may affect services tied to this program.

key_dates.label: Burnaby BAH Program Contract Renewal #3 Council vote date
Burnaby BAH Program Contract Renewal #3 term end date

briefs.title: Council to vote on $193,000 contract extension for seniors' housekeeping program
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on extending a contract with Comfort Keepers (0893967 B.C. Ltd.) worth an estimated $193,000 including taxes. The extension would keep housekeeping services running under the Better at Home Program — a BC government-funded program that helps seniors live independently — through December 31, 2026.
briefs.action_required: If you provide home support or senior care services, watch this contract decision — it signals ongoing city demand.

key_dates.label: Better at Home housekeeping contract extension end date — Burnaby

briefs.title: Council to Consider: $700,000 Increase to Archaeological Consulting Contract
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a pending contract change that would add $700,000 to an existing archaeological consulting services agreement, bringing the total estimated contract value to roughly $2.04 million over three years. Archaeological consulting involves studying and protecting historical artifacts and sites before or during construction — this change would be the second modification to a contract first awarded in September 2023.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — this could affect construction timelines or costs if your project requires archaeological review.

briefs.title: Council to vote on $700,000 contract extension for archaeological consulting services
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a $700,000 contract change (an increase to an existing agreement) with Campfire Archaeology and Heritage Ltd. to continue providing archaeological consulting services for City construction projects. The work helps the City follow BC's Heritage Conservation Act and supports its commitments to Truth and Reconciliation with Indigenous Peoples.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — this could affect construction timelines if your project requires archaeological review.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council meeting — archaeological consulting contract change vote

briefs.title: Council to Consider Adding 3 Toyota Tacoma Trucks to City Fleet
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a contract change that would add 3 more Toyota Tacoma pickup trucks to the City's fleet, at a cost of $174,384. This would use a 'piggyback' option — meaning the City would buy the trucks under the terms of an existing contract already in place — bringing the total spent on this truck purchase to $639,408.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision on February 10, 2026 — this is a public spending vote.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council vote — Toyota Tacoma fleet contract change pending approval

briefs.title: Council to vote on: Buying 3 more Toyota Tacoma trucks for $174,384
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a contract change that would add three Toyota Tacoma pickup trucks to a deal already in place with OpenRoad Toyota Richmond. The three new trucks — costing an estimated $174,384 including taxes — would replace aging city vehicles, which will be sold at auction once the new ones arrive.

briefs.title: Council to vote on $20M fuel supply contract with 4Refuel Canada LP
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on awarding a five-year contract worth up to $20 million (including taxes) to 4Refuel Canada LP for the supply and delivery of gasoline, diesel, biodiesel, and related fuel products to City facilities. The contract would run from March 11, 2026 to March 10, 2031, with the option to extend for two additional one-year periods if service and pricing remain satisfactory.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision on February 10, 2026 — this affects fuel supply to City operations.

key_dates.label: Burnaby fuel supply contract with 4Refuel Canada LP proposed start date

briefs.title: Council to vote on $648,206 garbage truck purchase for Burnaby
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on awarding a contract worth $648,206 (including taxes) to Rollins Machinery Ltd. for a new tandem axle front-load refuse truck — essentially a large commercial garbage truck. If approved, the new truck would replace an older unit that would then be sold at auction.

briefs.title: Council to vote on $621,838 contract for two new split body refuse trucks
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on awarding a contract worth $621,838 (including taxes) to Rollins Machinery Ltd. for the purchase of two Ford/HaulAll split body refuse trucks. These trucks would replace two aging city vehicles, which would then be sold at auction. The funding would come from the city's Equipment Replacement Reserve Fund.

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Lochdale School Park Playground Replacement Planned for 2026
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to consider site and context maps for a proposed playground replacement at Lochdale School Park, planned for 2026. The maps show the layout of the park, nearby schools, and surrounding green spaces, and appear to support an upcoming decision about upgrading aging playground equipment — some of which dates back to 1989.
briefs.action_required: Monitor Burnaby Council meeting agendas if your business is near Lochdale School Park for construction updates.

key_dates.label: Lochdale School Park playground replacement — planned start year

briefs.title: Council to Consider Permanently Closing Lochdale School Park Junior Playground
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on whether to permanently decommission the junior playground at Lochdale School Park and delay its replacement until future budget cycles. The playground was closed in December 2025 after it became unsafe and no longer met Canadian Standards Association safety rules. Staff recommend deferring any replacement — which could cost between $300,000 and $1 million — until funding is available through the City's five-year capital budget process.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision if your business or community group uses Lochdale School Park.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council vote — Lochdale School Park playground future decision

briefs.title: Council to vote on: Burnaby Procedure Bylaw update for advisory body oversight
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on amendments to the Burnaby Procedure Bylaw 2024 that would clarify how the rules (called 'Terms of Reference') for the City's advisory bodies are created and updated. Under the proposed changes, the Mayor would direct the preparation of these rules, which would then be reviewed by a Council committee before going to the full Council for approval.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the public notice posted on Burnaby's website and eNewsletter to see the proposed changes.

briefs.title: Proposed: Restaurant Use at 8340 Fraser Reach Court, Burnaby — Council to Consider
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to consider a proposal to allow a restaurant use at 8340 Fraser Reach Court. The site sketch, prepared by the Planning and Development Department, identifies a space of approximately 2,217 square feet (205.97 m²) at this location as the subject site for the proposed restaurant use.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — this could affect nearby food service businesses or your development plans.
briefs.industries: Food Service, Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt

briefs.title: Council to Consider Removing Restaurant-Only Restriction at Burnaby Industrial Site
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on removing a legal restriction (called a covenant) that currently requires a 2,217 sq. ft. space at 8340 Fraser Reach Court to be used only as a restaurant. The property owner, Oxford Properties, has tried for years to find a restaurant tenant with no success, so staff are recommending the restriction be lifted so the space can be used for light industrial purposes instead.
briefs.action_required: If you are interested in leasing this space as a restaurant or for industrial use, watch for the council decision.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council meeting — covenant discharge vote at 8340 Fraser Reach Court

briefs.industries: Food Service, Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to vote on new zoning districts and updated land-use rules
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a package of amendments to the city's Zoning Bylaw that would create five new zoning districts — including Employment (E1), Industrial (I1), Parks & Natural Areas (PM1), Institutional (PM2), and Agricultural (AM1) — and update dozens of definitions and rules to support the OCP 2050 land-use plan. If approved, the changes would affect what businesses can operate where, how buildings are measured and built, and how parking, fencing, screening, and temporary uses are regulated across the city.
briefs.action_required: Review the proposed zoning changes to see if your property or business use is affected before Council votes.
briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Agriculture

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to vote on new zoning rules for industrial, parks, and farm land
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed zoning bylaw that would create or revise rules for five land-use zones: Employment (E1), Industrial (I1), Parks & Open Spaces (PM1), Institutional (PM2), and Agriculture (AM1). The proposed bylaw would set out what types of businesses can operate in each zone, how buildings must be sized and placed on a lot, and special conditions for uses like cannabis facilities, animal care, crematoria, and affordable rental housing near SkyTrain stations.
briefs.action_required: Check which zone your property falls under — these proposed rules could affect what you can build or operate.

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to vote on five new non-residential zoning districts
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on proposed changes to Burnaby's Zoning Bylaw that would create five new zoning districts — areas with specific rules about what kinds of businesses and uses are allowed — to support the city's long-term land use plan, Burnaby 2050. The proposed rules affect employment, industrial, agricultural, and other non-residential zones, including new restrictions on where storage facilities can operate and updated rules for recycling and waste processing sites.
briefs.action_required: Check if your business is in an affected zone — new rules could change what uses are allowed on your property.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Zoning Bylaw non-residential district amendments — Council vote

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Vancouver Fraser Port Authority Update on Second Narrows Dredging Works
briefs.summary: The Vancouver Fraser Port Authority is set to appear before Burnaby Mayor and Council on February 10, 2026, to give an update on dredging works at Second Narrows. Dredging means removing sediment from the bottom of a waterway to keep it deep enough for ships. Representatives will give a short slide presentation and answer questions from Council.
briefs.action_required: Attend the February 10, 2026 Council meeting if the Second Narrows dredging works affect your business.

key_dates.label: Vancouver Fraser Port Authority Second Narrows dredging update — Burnaby Council presentation

briefs.industries: Transportation & Logistics, Construction & Development, Natural Resources

briefs.title: Burnaby Council Approves 2026–2030 Financial Plan and Multiple City Contracts
briefs.summary: At its January 27, 2026 meeting, Burnaby City Council voted on several major items, including approving a five-year financial plan (2026–2030) and passing a related bylaw, awarding construction and service contracts totalling over $16 million, and referring an anti-Palestinian racism framework to staff for further review. Business owners in construction, facilities, and property sectors should take note of the new contracts and housing agreement changes approved at this meeting.
briefs.action_required: Review the approved contracts and housing bylaw changes to see if your business or property is affected.
briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Transportation & Logistics

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Zoning Bylaw Rewrite Would Reshape Rules for Employment, Industrial & Agricultural Land
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to consider a major rewrite of its Zoning Bylaw — the rulebook that controls what businesses can operate where. The proposed changes would cut the number of zoning categories from 53 down to 15, add new restrictions on self-storage facilities near SkyTrain stations, allow limited housing on employment lands near transit, and update rules for farms, institutions, and industrial sites.
briefs.action_required: Review the proposed zoning changes at the Burnaby Zoning Bylaw Rewrite website to see how your business location may be affected.
